questions.q: What does the presentation $G = \langle S \mid R \rangle$ formally denote?
choices: The free group on $S$ with extra generators $R$
The quotient $F(S) / \langle\!\langle R \rangle\!\rangle$ of the free group on $S$ by the normal closure of $R$
The direct product of cyclic groups generated by $S$, modulo the relations $R$
The Cayley graph with vertex set $S$ and edge set $R$
questions.explain: A presentation realizes $G$ as $F(S) / N$, where $N$ is the smallest normal subgroup of $F(S)$ containing $R$ — this guarantees every word in $R$ becomes trivial in $G$ along with all conjugates.

questions.q: Which group is $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle$?
choices: The free group on two generators $F_2$
questions.explain: The relator $aba^{-1}b^{-1} = e$ says $ab = ba$, so adding commutativity to the free group on $\{a,b\}$ yields $\mathbb{Z}^2$.

questions.q: What is the word problem for a finitely-presented group?
choices: Decide whether two words in the generators give the same element
Compute the order of every element
Construct an explicit Cayley graph drawing
questions.explain: Equivalently, decide whether a given word equals the identity. Novikov (1955) and Boone (1958) showed this is undecidable in general, but solvable for many large classes (hyperbolic, automatic, residually finite F.P.).

gcgt-cayley-graph.title: Cayley graphs and the word metric

questions.q: In $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$, what is the degree of every vertex when $S$ is symmetric (i.e. $s \in S \Rightarrow s^{-1} \in S$) and contains no involutions?
questions.explain: Each $s \in S$ contributes one outgoing edge $g \to gs$ at every vertex. With $S$ symmetric and involution-free, edges come in $\{s, s^{-1}\}$ pairs, so each vertex has exactly $|S|$ neighbors.

questions.q: Which action of $G$ on $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ is by graph automorphisms?
questions.explain: Left multiplication preserves edges: if $h \to hs$ is an edge, then $gh \to ghs = (gh)s$ is also an edge. Right multiplication moves along the edge labelled $s$, so it is not an automorphism (it permutes labels).

questions.q: What does the word metric $d_S(g, h)$ measure?
choices: The number of subgroups separating $g$ and $h$
The length of the shortest word in $S^{\pm 1}$ representing $g^{-1}h$
The Euclidean distance between $g$ and $h$ in some embedding
questions.explain: $d_S(g, h)$ is the graph distance in $\mathrm{Cay}(G, S)$ — equivalently, the smallest $n$ such that $g^{-1}h = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_n$ with each $s_i \in S \cup S^{-1}$.

questions.q: Which of the following is a quasi-isometry between $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$?
questions.explain: $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is a $(1,1)$-quasi-isometry: $|\lfloor x \rfloor - \lfloor y \rfloor| \le |x-y| + 1$ and image is $1$-dense. Constants collapse distance; exponentials blow it up.

questions.q: By the Schwarz–Milnor lemma, two finite generating sets $S, S'$ of the same group $G$ produce Cayley graphs that are:
questions.explain: Each generator of $S'$ has finite length in $S$, giving a bilipschitz comparison of word metrics — hence a quasi-isometry. The graphs are usually not isomorphic: $\mathbb{Z}$ with $S=\{1\}$ is a line, with $S=\{2,3\}$ it has different local structure.

questions.q: Which property is NOT a quasi-isometry invariant of finitely-generated groups?
choices: The order of a specific element
questions.explain: Element order is local arithmetic — invisible to large-scale geometry. Growth, ends, and hyperbolicity are all coarse invariants.

questions.q: What is the growth type of $\mathbb{Z}^d$ with the standard generating set?
questions.explain: The ball of radius $n$ in $\mathbb{Z}^d$ is a discrete diamond (the $\ell^1$-ball) of volume $\Theta(n^d)$ — polynomial growth of degree exactly $d$.

questions.q: What is the growth type of the free group $F_2$?
questions.explain: The Cayley graph of $F_2 = \langle a, b \rangle$ is the $4$-regular tree, whose ball of radius $n$ has $1 + 4(3^n - 1)/2$ vertices — exponential growth with rate $3$.

questions.q: What does Gromov's polynomial-growth theorem say?
choices: Every f.g. group has polynomial growth
A f.g. group has polynomial growth iff it is virtually nilpotent
Polynomial growth is equivalent to being abelian
questions.explain: Gromov (1981): polynomial growth $\Leftrightarrow$ a finite-index nilpotent subgroup exists. The hard direction uses an asymptotic-cone limit argument and Montgomery–Zippin's solution to Hilbert's fifth problem.

questions.q: A geodesic triangle in a $\delta$-hyperbolic space is $\delta$-thin in the sense that:
choices: Each side has length at most $\delta$
Each side lies in the $\delta$-neighborhood of the union of the other two
The triangle has area at most $\delta$
Some pair of sides is parallel up to $\delta$
questions.explain: Thin-triangles capture negative curvature combinatorially: in $\mathbb{H}^2$ all triangles satisfy this with a uniform $\delta$, while in $\mathbb{R}^2$ no finite $\delta$ works.

questions.q: Which of the following is hyperbolic?
questions.explain: Trees are $0$-hyperbolic, and the Cayley graph of $F_n$ is a $2n$-regular tree. Abelian groups of rank $\ge 2$ contain flat planes and are not hyperbolic.

questions.q: Why is the word problem solvable in linear time for a hyperbolic group?
choices: Because every word equal to the identity admits a Dehn presentation: it contains more than half of some relator, allowing greedy shortening
Because every element has finite order
questions.explain: Dehn's algorithm — repeatedly replacing more-than-half of a relator with the shorter complement — terminates linearly in word length on a hyperbolic group, decisively solving the word problem.

questions.q: Mostow's rigidity theorem says that for closed hyperbolic manifolds of dimension $\ge 3$:
choices: Isomorphism of fundamental groups implies isometry (up to scale)
questions.explain: A homotopy equivalence between two closed hyperbolic $n$-manifolds ($n \ge 3$) is homotopic to an isometry. So $\pi_1$ determines the metric — a striking failure of flexibility in higher rank.

questions.q: Cayley graphs of which family give an explicit construction of expander graphs?
choices: $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with a fixed finite generating set (varying $p$)
Any sequence of finite cyclic groups
questions.explain: Margulis (1973) showed Cayley graphs of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with a fixed generating set form an expander family — a key application of property (T) and a foundation of pseudorandom-graph constructions.

questions.q: Which decision problem for finitely-presented groups is undecidable in full generality?
choices: All three: word, conjugacy, and isomorphism problems
questions.explain: Novikov–Boone (word), Miller (conjugacy), and Adian–Rabin (isomorphism) all proved undecidability for general f.p. groups. Each becomes tractable on restricted classes — hyperbolic groups, for instance, decide all three.
